Computer-Assisted Reporting Newsletter, 1990–2007

Browse Topics Search

Measuring foster care

Jason Method Asbury Park Press January/February 2001
Investigative Methods

Despite a significant reform effort by New Jersey's child protection agency, children are spending more time in foster care. The article explores the state's lack of progress against its reform goals.

Despite New Jersey's child protection agency's two-year, $128 million reform effort, children who are wards of the state are spending more time in foster care, group homes and institutions than just a few years ago. That was the key finding when the Asbury Park Press decided to find out how much, if any, progress the state had made in its reform goals. The child protection agency is veiled under confidentiality laws, and New Jersey has one of the weakest public access laws in the nation.

DYFS officials turned down requests for statistical reports. Fortunately, a federal database helped pierce through this confidentiality. The Adoption and Foster Care Analysis and Reporting System (AFCARS) tracks 600,000 children under state foster care. This database includes detailed information on each child such as birth date, sex, race, disabilities, entry and exit dates from care, reasons for removal, and more.

Despite confidentiality, AFCARS data provides essential insights, such as the number of placements and removals from homes. Unfortunately, the data has its limitations like missing details on some critical aspects. However, as AFCARS data from 1999 and 2000 was obtained, it demonstrated that New Jersey's system wasn't significantly reformed yet, and black children seemingly suffer worse care. This evaluation in SPSS led an impactful report showing stagnancy in reform efforts.
